text: The surgeon targets the superior hemorrhoidal artery, located distal to the left colic artery, in a patient with a long redundant sigmoid colon. A ureter is visible during the procedure. The specimen is exteriorized through the ostomy site marked by a 12mm trocar in arm three.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery just distal to the left colic using scissors for dissection. A small window is created distal to the take-off of the left colic, preparing to ligate the vessel with a ligature. The focus is on the TME dissection in a narrow male pelvis post-neoadjuvant therapy, with the surgeon entering the lateral plane and taking down the white line.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection by checking the ureter and using electrocautery on the right side, staying within the mesorectal envelope along the holy plane. The left side is identified, and the surgeon joins the medial and lateral planes, using a sponge to visualize the lateral aspect of the TME plane.

The surgeon begins the posterior dissection, using arm number one to push up and arm number two for retraction and grasping. They work circumferentially downwards, noting the proximal ICG tattoo and proceeding below the peritoneal reflection in a narrow male pelvis. The ureter is checked, and the surgeon scores the peritoneum to join the anterior dissection, using it as a guide for further dissection.

The surgeon manipulates the robotic arms to navigate the dissection, noting the potential for the arms to collide. The posterior dissection reveals edematous tissues due to radiation, generating smoke. The surgeon switches to a suction device in arm number one to clear smoke and fluid. Anterior dissection continues, with the distal tattoo visible, and the surgeon aims to dissect a few centimeters below it.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to arm two for a better view as the right arm number four encounters the iliac. The stapler is prepared to be introduced through arm three, while scissors are positioned in arm three and suction in arm four. The surgeon checks the dissection below the tumor using a finger and rigid scope before stapling.

The surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to grasp and achieve hemostasis while coning in on the mesorectum just above the levator floor. The distal tattoo is visible below the peritoneal reflection, and the surgeon aims to dissect three to five centimeters below it.

The surgeon begins amputating the mesorectum just below the pelvic floor, leaving a small portion for tumor-specific excision. The tumor is located approximately four centimeters from the anorectal ring. The surgeon positions the stapler to staple only the bowel, avoiding the mesorectum, using an anterior to posterior approach. The stapler is placed from arm three, ensuring no tumor is felt or visible.

The surgeon places the anvil through the ileostomy site and checks for hemostasis, addressing a small area of bleeding. The rectal stump is observed retracting into the levator floor after transection. The surgeon positions the anvil in the pelvis, mates the stapler to the anvil, and fires the staple while ensuring the ureter is not affected.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, which is positioned low due to the patient's radiation treatment. The procedure includes a diverting loop to ensure proper healing.
